summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on John Maeda's 'The Laws of Simplicity' and its relevance to software development. He argues that programmers become so accustomed to complexity that they unconsciously build it into their work. New programmers default to creating impossibly complex applications, while only experienced developers can produce truly simple, understandable software. The core message is that simplicity requires active, deliberate effort rather than passive intention.
key_points: Programmers become desensitized to complexity because they're constantly immersed in it
Complex applications are the default output for inexperienced programmers
Only seasoned developers have the skill to produce genuinely simple software
Simplicity must be actively pursued as a force, not passively hoped for
Maintaining simplicity feels like 'back-breaking work' against entropy
money_quotes: Programmers swim in a sea of unending complexity.
We get so used to complexity as an ambient norm that we begin, consciously or unconsciously, projecting it into our work.
Impossibly complex applications are the default deliverable for new programmers.
Only seasoned software development veterans are capable of producing applications that are easy to understand and troubleshoot.
Simplicity isn't achievable as a passive goal; it's a force that must be actively applied.
Keep it Simple, Stupid. If only it was that easy.
It feels more like back-breaking work to keep things from inevitably devolving into complexity.
key_insight: Simplicity in software isn't a natural state or passive goal—it's a force that must be actively and continuously applied against the gravitational pull of complexity.
